project_description.text: Metropolitan Dade County proposes a 20.5-mile heavy rail rapid transit system (Stage I) running from Dadeland in the south through downtown Miami to Okeechobee in Hialeah, with about 18 miles elevated and 2.5 miles at-grade. The system includes 20-21 stations (most with parking), 166 new rail cars using semi-automated duo-rail technology, expanded feeder bus service, and a yard and shop facility at Site H-1 in Hialeah. Total estimated capital cost is about $795 million, with construction planned from 1978-1979 through 1982-1983 and supported by a UMTA federal capital grant.

affected_community.text: The corridor passes through diverse communities of Dade County (1976 population about 1,449,300), including South Miami, Coral Gables, downtown Miami, Hialeah, Miami Springs, and low-to-moderate income neighborhoods such as Culmer, Allapattah, and the Model Cities area. The county has a substantial Spanish-American population (about 32%), a sizable black community (about 15%), and many transit-dependent elderly residents. Project displacements are estimated at 575-650 residential units and 100-150 businesses, falling disproportionately on black households (about 58%) and Latin households (about 31%), with 14 businesses displaced at the H-1 yard site.

alternatives_overview.text: Planners evaluated 41 systemwide networks, narrowing them to a Null (no-build) option, low- and high-cost All Bus alternatives, and several fixed-guideway rail options of varying scale. A Priority Engineering and Operational Analyses (PEOA) process then examined 30 corridor scenarios using conventional rail, light rail, and streetcar technology, advancing six finalists (A-1, A-2, A-4, A-7, A-8, and G-1(B)); Alternative A-7 was selected and adopted by the County Commission in October 1976. Eighteen yard and shop sites were screened down to seven finalists, with Site H-1 in Hialeah ultimately chosen, and monorail technology was studied but rejected on capacity, safety, and cost grounds.

environmental_impact.text: The rail system is expected to reduce daily auto travel by roughly 1 million vehicle miles, lowering carbon monoxide, hydrocarbon, and nitrogen oxide emissions, conserving energy, and avoiding an estimated 15 fatal and 1,800 injury accidents per year, while creating about 3,000 construction and 1,600-1,800 operating jobs. Adverse impacts include displacement of hundreds of homes and businesses, visual intrusion from elevated structures, noise that may exceed APTA guidelines near some residences and parks, vegetation loss, and removal of about 358 acres from the tax rolls. Mitigation includes sound barriers, landscaping, retention basins, grass swales, oil and washwater controls at the H-1 yard near the Hialeah-Miami Springs wellfield, and design measures to protect three National Register-eligible historic properties.

public_response.text: The project was shaped by extensive public involvement, including approval of a $132.5 million transit bond issue in 1972, roughly 500 public meetings during Preliminary Engineering, 24 public forums, citizen panels, and a February 6, 1978 public hearing on the Draft EIS. Comments from federal, state, and local agencies, environmental groups (Sierra Club, Tropical Audubon Society, American Lung Association), citizen organizations (STOP, Taxpayers League, League of Women Voters), and individual residents raised concerns about ridership and cost projections, displacement in the black community, noise and visual impacts, water quality near wellfields, and the adequacy of alternatives. Strong neighborhood opposition led to dropping the Kendall and Glenwood yard sites in favor of the compromise H-1 site, while a March 1978 referendum reaffirmed support for the balanced transportation system.

value.name: Alternative A-1 (Perrine to NW 65th Street)
value.description: Fully grade-separated conventional rail running approximately 23 miles from SW 184th Street/US 1 in Perrine to NW 65th Street/NW 27th Avenue. Includes 34,100 ft at-grade and 87,000 ft elevated double track, 23 stations, and the Cutler Ridge yard and shop site.

value.name: Alternative A-2 (Perrine to Civic Center)
value.description: Fully grade-separated conventional rail running approximately 19 miles from SW 184th Street/US 1 in Perrine to the Civic Center area, with 18 stations, 34,100 ft at-grade and 64,900 ft elevated track, and the Cutler Ridge yard and shop site.

value.name: Alternative A-4 (SW 144th Street to NW 65th Street)
value.description: Fully grade-separated conventional rail (with dual power pickup for yard access) running approximately 20 miles from SW 144th Street/US 1 to NW 65th Street/NW 27th Avenue, with 21 stations, 26,100 ft at-grade and 80,300 ft elevated track, and the Cutler Ridge yard and shop site.

value.name: Alternative A-7 (Dadeland South to West 8th Ave/Hialeah)
value.description: Fully grade-separated conventional rail running approximately 20.5 miles from the Dadeland South area to West 8th Avenue in Hialeah, with 22 stations, 18,300 ft at-grade and 90,000 ft elevated track, and the Hialeah yard and shop site. This alternative was adopted as the recommended plan.

value.name: Alternative A-8 (Dadeland South to Miami-Dade College North)
value.description: Fully grade-separated conventional rail running from the Dadeland South area to NW 119th Street/NW 27th Avenue, with 22 stations, 18,300 ft at-grade and 87,700 ft elevated track, and the Opa Locka yard and shop site.

value.name: Alternative G-1(B) (Streetcar/Light Rail – Perrine to NW 65th Street)
value.description: Standard Light Rail Vehicle (SLRV) operating at-grade throughout the South Dixie Highway corridor from Perrine to the Miami River (82,000 ft at-grade), then fully grade-separated north of the river to NW 65th Street (38,100 ft elevated), with 40 stations total and no traffic signal preemption. Uses the Cutler Ridge yard and shop site.

subthemes: Urban Expansion and Land Use Change

themes.justification: The project centers on a heavy rail rapid transit system with extensive elevated structures affecting urban landscapes and mobility networks, while the displacement of 575-650 residential units disproportionately impacting Black and Latin households, combined with organized community opposition (STOP, neighborhood groups) that reshaped yard site selection, directly invokes land rights/displacement and community resistance subthemes.

places.name: Dadeland, FL to Okeechobee Station, Hialeah, FL

geocoded.name: Dadeland, FL to Okeechobee Station, Hialeah, FL

location.note: Rail rapid transit project in Metropolitan Dade County, Florida. The 20.5-mile route runs from Dadeland (southwest of Miami) northeasterly along the FEC Railroad right-of-way through downtown Miami, then north to NW 79th Street and through Hialeah to the Okeechobee Station terminal.
